r  Nuclear binding energies, often represented instead
a
via the atomic mass excess ME(Z, A) for a nucleus with
Z protons and A nucleons, are a key ingredient in calcu-
lations of astrophysical burning environments (H. Schatz
2013; H. Schatz & W. J. Ong 2017; Z. Meisel 2020). A
nuclear reaction’s energetic favorability is represented by
the Q-value, where for radiative proton-capture (p, γ) re-
actions Qp,γ(Z, A) = ME(Z, A)+MEp −ME(Z +1, A+
1) ≡ Sp(Z + 1, A + 1), where Sp is the proton separa-

with updated nuclear data to identify a possible
re of the rapid-proton capture (rp-) process that
ars. We find that the rp-process flow through the
relevant conditions. Our results call for new studies
on emission branching, and nuclear structure as it
rst temperatures.
tion energy. The rapid proton capture (rp-)process that
powers type-I x-ray bursts on accreting neutron star sur-
faces is a nuclear burning sequence consisting primarily
+
of (p, γ) reactions and β  -decays proceeding up the nu-
clear landscape following Sp ∼ 1 MeV near the proton-
drip line from roughly isotopes of calcium up to as far as
isotopes of tin (Z. Meisel et al. 2018). The near-constant
Sp of the rp-process path is due to the high temperature,
6  −3
T ∼ 1 GK, and hydrogen density, ρH ∼ 10 mol cm  ,
of the environment, which enables proton captures along
an isotonic chain until (p, γ)−(γ, p) equilibrium is estab-
lished.  At that point, nucleosynthesis stalls until the
equilibrium nucleus undergoes radioactive decay. Equi-
librium nuclei with half-lives t  ≳10 s are known as
1/2
waiting-point nuclei and are largely responsible for the
morphology of the x-ray burst light curve temporal peak
and decay in luminosity (S. E. Woosley et al. 2004).
Recent nuclear mass measurements and nuclear struc-
64
ture  calculations  established that  Ge  is  a  strong
waiting-point  (Y.  H.  Lam et  al.  2016;  X.  Zhou
et al.  2023), meaning that a negligible  fraction of
the rp-process flow proceeds  through proton-capture
65
on  the  abundance  of  As  that  is  present  due
64  65  64  64  ⇆65
to  Ge(p, γ) As(γ, p) Ge equilibrium ( Ge  As).
66
Notably, these same measurements established that  Se
is more bound than previous theoretical predictions (X.
Zhou et al. 2023).
Here, we show that translating this assumption of in-
65
creased binding to the neighboring isotope  Se, which
is well within current estimates (W. Huang et al. 2021;
M. Wang et al. 2021), opens a possible bypass of the
64
Ge rp-process waiting point akin to that recently es-
56
tablished for  Ni (O. Forstner et al. 2001; C. Langer

The  possible  nuclear  reaction  sequences  of  the
64
rp-process  near  Ge  are  shown  in Fig.  1.  In
the  standard  path,  the  rp-process  proceeds  as
63  + 63  64  + 64  65  66
Ge(β  ) Ga(p, γ) Ge(β  ) Ga(p, γ) Ge(p, γ) As.
Flow through the waiting-point would instead consist of
63  + 63  64  65  66  + 66
Ge(β  ) Ga(p, γ) Ge(p, γ) As(p, γ)  Se(β  ) As.
For  a  bypass,  the  relevant  reaction  sequence  is
63  64  65  + 65  66  + 66
Ge(p, γ) As(p, γ)  Se(β  ) As(p, γ)  Se(β  ) As.
65
However, the bypass can be thwarted by  Se decaying
64
via β-delayed proton (βp-)emission to  Ge,  which
is presently estimated to occur with a decay branch
65  +12
probability Pβp(  Se) = 88−13% (A. M. Rogers et al.
2011).
The portion of  rp-process flow proceeding through
the bypass  is  set by the  competition between the
63
Ge β-decay  rate λ63Ge(β+)  and  the  rate  for  the
63  64  65
Ge(p, γ) As(p, γ)  Se reaction sequence λ2p.  The
dynamics of  this reaction sequence  is set by T ,  ρH,
64  65
Sp( As),  and Sp(  Se).  For  each of  these  reac-
tions,  (p, γ)−(γ, p)  equilibrium is  established when
2  3/2  ≳
(ρHNA/nγ)(µredc /(kBT))  exp(−Sp/(kBT))  1,
according to the Saha equation, where µred  is  the

reduced mass, nγ = π(kBT) /(13c  ) is the number
density of photons according to the Planck distribution,
and NA,  kB,  and ℏ are the Avogadro, Boltzmann,
and  reduced  Planck  constants,  respectively  (Z.
64  65
Meisel  et  al.  2018).  Because Sp( As)≪Sp(  Se),
63  ⇆64
Ge  As equilibrium is established at at  lower T
64  ⇆65
than  required  to  establish  As  Se  equilibrium.
63  64
When only  Ge and  As are  in equilibrium,  the

63

2  −3/2  64  64
λ2p,one  ∝  ρHT  exp(Sp( As)/(kBT))⟨ As(p, γ)⟩,
64  64
where  ⟨ As(p, γ)⟩  is  the  As(p, γ)  thermonuclear
reaction rate (H. Schatz et al.  1998).  When both
of  these reactions are  in equilibrium,  the rate  from
63
Ge into the bypass reaction sequence is λ2p,both ∝
2  −3  64  65
ρ T  exp((Sp( As)  +  Sp(  Se))/(kBT))λ65Se(β+),
H

where λ65Se(β+)  is the  Se β  -decay rate (H. Schatz
et al. 1998).
The boundary in T−ρH phase space where λ2p,both ap-
plies is shown by the dashed-red lines of Fig. 2. Above
this boundary, no bypass is expected, as abundances
63  64  65
along the  Ge(p, γ) As(p, γ)  Se reaction sequence are
statistically distributed.  Below this boundary, λ2p,one
applies. However, the flow through the bypass will only
be significant if λ2p,one≳λ63  + , which is the bound-
Ge(β  )
ary indicated by the solid-blue lines of Fig. 2.  Sig-
nificance of the bypass additionally requires that the
65  65  + 65
amount of  As created from  Se(β  ) As is large rela-
64  ⇆65
tive to the  Ge  As equilibrium abundance. Recent
results show that this equilibrium abundance is small,
but non-zero (X. Zhou et al. 2023).  Meanwhile,  the
maximum possible bypass fraction for any region of the
65
T − ρH phase space is 1 − Pβp(  Se).

64
In order to explore the  Ge bypass physics further, we
performed flow calculations following the approach pre-
56
viously employed for the  Ni bypass (W. J. Ong et al.
2017; M. Saxena et al. 2022). A limited nuclear reac-
tion network including all p, α, and γ-induced reactions
+
as well as all β  and βp-decays on all isotopes shown in
Fig. 1 was initialized with the entire non-hydrogen abun-
63
dance concentrated in  Ge. The network was numeri-
cally solved at constant T and ρH across the phase-space
thought to be relevant for the diverse set of possible x-
ray burst conditions (A. Parikh et al. 2008), shown in
Fig. 2. Steady-state abundances were determined by al-
lowing the reactions to propagate for 120 s. The ratio
66  +
of the total flux of  Se(β  ) to the summed total flux of
66  +  65  +  64  +
Se(β  ),  As(β  ), and  Ge(β  ) was used to measure
the fraction of rp-process flow proceeding through the
64
Ge waiting-point bypass.
Nuclear data used in the calculations  includes the
nuclear reaction rates of the latest REACLIB (R. H.
Cyburt  et al.  2010)  snapshot  (from 24 June 2021)
updated with recently measured nuclear masses  (X.
Zhou et al. 2023) and βp-branchings (A. M. Rogers
et al. 2011), where nuclear masses were used to cal-
culate consistent forward (p, γ) and reverse (γ, p) re-
action rates (T. Rauscher & F.-K. Thielemann 2000).
Guided by the bypass physics, we  investigated the

64
Calculated rp-process flows through the  Ge waiting-
point bypass are shown in Fig. 2.  The bypass begins
to open for the favorable nuclear physics assumptions
and achieves a maximum of 36% for the maximally fa-
vorable nuclear physics assumptions.  The contour of
the T − ρH phase space in which the bypass  is sig-
nificant is set by the previously discussed equilibrium
conditions. An enhancement in the nuclear binding of
65  64
Se makes  As(p, γ) relevant at lower ρH and higher

3
Ge waiting-point bypass, as indicated by an inverted black-body
ht-panel) maximally favorable nuclear physics input. The red
he blue solid line is the boundary at which λ2p,one=λ63Ge(β+).
64
T , while an enhancement of ⟨ As(p, γ)⟩ improves the
63  64  65  63  +
competition of  Ge(p, γ) As(p, γ)  Se with  Ge(β  ).
Even for the most favorable assumptions and astrophys-
65
ical conditions, the flow cannot exceed 1-Pβp(  Se), as
65
Se(βp) re-routes flow from the bypass into the stan-
dard rp-process path.
56
Relative to the  Ni waiting-point bypass (M. Saxena
64
et al. 2022), the bypass around the  Ge waiting-point is
potentially twice as strong. However, significant flow re-
quires plausible but somewhat more extreme ρH. This is
due to the higher Coulomb barriers involved, as well as
64  56
the much-shorter decay half-life of  Ge relative to  Ni.
The astrophysical conditions with >2% bypass shown in
Fig. 2 are more hydrogen-rich than thought to be rele-
vant for the often-studied clock-burster system (G. Merz
& Z. Meisel 2021), but are observed in calculations ex-
ploring a wider range of x-ray bursting system param-
eters (H. Schatz & W. J. Ong 2017; I. Sultana et al.
2022). Determining the full astrophysical implications
for these and other x-ray bursting systems will require
detailed studies coupling lower and higher-dimensional
astrophysics model calculations with model-observation
comparisons. While our results indicate that such stud-
ies are worthwhile, these involve considerable effort in
their own right (e.g. R. H. Cyburt et al. 2016; Z. Meisel
2018; Z. Meisel et al. 2022) and are left for future work.
Based on prior work investigating nuclear physics im-
pacts for the Cu-Ni cycle (Z. Meisel et al. 2019), we an-
ticipate that potential impacts could include a modified
shape of the tail of the burst light curve, reducing the

64
Further  refining the bypass  fraction for  the  Ge
waiting-point will require dedicated studies in nuclear
experiment and theory.  In particular, the present work
65
calls for a higher-precision measurement of Pβp(  Se),
65
experimental determination of ME(  Se) with precision
O(100 keV), and experimental as well as theoretical con-
65
straints on the nuclear structure of  Se required to cal-
64
culate the  As(p, γ) reaction rate.
64  65
The existence of the  Ge bypass relies on Pβp(  Se) <
100%. This is not only within the present experimen-
tal uncertainty (A. M. Rogers et al. 2011), but would
be  in-line with systematics.  Nuclides with isospin-
projection TZ = (A − 2Z)/2 = −3/2 and A = 4n − 1
are expected to have similar βp-decay properties, par-
ticularly when they are within the same harmonic os-
cillator  shell  (here fp,  i.e.  Z = 20 − 40)  (J. C.
Batchelder et al. 1993; J. C. Batchelder 2020). Nearby
57  61  69  73
nuclides in this group,  Zn,  Ge,  Kr, and  Sr, have
+1
Pβp = 84.7±1.4%, 62±4%, 99−11%, and ∼100%, respec-
tively (M. Saxena et al. 2022; J. C. Batchelder 2020).
+
However, it should be noted that the β  -decay children
69  73
of  Kr and  Sr are significantly more proton unbound
65
(Sp < −600 keV (M. Wang et al. 2021)) relative to  As
(Sp = −220±40 keV (X. Zhou et al. 2023)) and are thus
extremely unstable to proton emission.
65
Enhanced nuclear binding of  Se relative to a smooth
nuclear mass surface is also key for the existence of the
64
Ge bypass. Recent mass measurements of the nearby
64  66
isotopes  As and  Se found that these are more bound
than the standard smooth extrapolation of the nuclear
mass surface (W. Huang et al. 2021; M. Wang et al.
2021) by 180 keV and 320 keV, respectively (X. Zhou
et al. 2023). These each correspond to approximately 1σ
in nuclear binding enhancement.  Additionally,  some-
65
what enhanced binding for  Se is indicated when as-
suming isospin mirror symmetry. Empirically, fp-shell
nuclides display a smooth trend in the Coulomb dis-
placement energy CDE, which is a measure of isospin
symmetry breaking due to the proton-neutron charge
difference (H. F. Li et al. 2024). Following the approach
65
of A. M. Rogers et al. (2011) by applying the  As–
65  65
Ge CDE (H. F. Li et al. 2024) to the  As binding
energy, correcting for the difference between empirical
CDE trends for  isospin 1/2 and 3/2 nuclides (M. S.
65
Antony et al. 1997), ME(  Se) ≈ −33100 keV. A caveat
is that this estimate could be impacted by deviations
from the smooth CDE trend due to odd-even stagger-
ing in Z (E. Feenberg & G. Goertzel 1946), as well as

any mirror symmetry breaking (e.g. D. E. M. Hoff et al.
2020).
65
Whether the maximum bypass fraction (1-Pβp(  Se))
is achieved for astrophysically relevant conditions de-
64
pends largely upon ⟨ As(p, γ)⟩. For a ×100 enhance-
ment over the REACLIB rate (R. H. Cyburt et al. 2010)
65
and maximum  Se nuclear binding, the bypass fraction
can reach 36%, while for a ×10 rate enhancement the
bypass fraction only achieves 14%.  In the absence of
experimental constraints, plausibility of these rate en-
hancements can only be evaluated by comparison to
other theoretical predictions, as performed in Fig. 3.
The REACLIB rate is based on the statistical model
using the Hauser-Feshbach formalism.  Other  com-
monly adopted Hauser-Feshbach estimates (T. Rauscher
& F.-K. Thielemann 2000; A. Koning et al. 2023) for
64
⟨ As(p, γ)⟩ are within ×3 of the REACLIB estimate,
where differences are primarily due  to assumptions
regarding the γ-strength function and direct capture
mechanism (B. Wang et al. 2024). However, a resonant
rate approximation may be more valid given the rela-
tively low nuclear level density for the excitation ener-
65
gies expected to be populated within  Se at the relevant
T (T. Rauscher et al. 1997). For this approximation, a
near-maximal rate estimate results from placing a sin-
gle resonance at the optimal resonance energy Er (J. R.
Newton et al. 2007) (≈1 MeV in the present case) with a
resonance strength near the Wigner single-particle limit
ωγWig (T. Teichmann & E. P. Wigner 1952).  In prac-
tice, even a very strong resonance strength may only
be tens-of-percent of the Wigner limit, for instance as
estimated for the strongest resonances of the nearby rp-
64  65
process reactions  Ge(p, γ) and  As(p, γ) (Y. H. Lam
et al. 2016). Resonant reaction rates calculated (G. Merz
& Z. Meisel 2021) with these assumptions result in a
×≈10–100 enhancement near the T≈0.8 GK tempera-
64
ture at which the  Ge waiting-point bypass fraction is
estimated to be largest.

We performed astrophysics model calculations of the
64
reaction flow near the  Ge waiting point of the rp-
process that powers x-ray bursts on accreting neutron
stars. Based on recently updated nuclear data near the
proton dripline, we implemented somewhat enhanced
65
nuclear binding of  Se and find that this alters the
textbook understanding of  the rp-process nuclear re-
action sequence.  For the most favorable astrophysical
64
conditions and nuclear physics assumptions, the  Ge
waiting-point may be bypassed for up to 36% of the rp-
process flow.  Our results call  for new studies of this
exotic region of isospin asymmetry in the nuclear land-

timates to the statistical model estimate in REACLIB (R. H.
Cyburt et al. 2010). Ratios for statistical model rates from
Non-SMOKER (T. Rauscher & F.-K. Thielemann 2000) and

(red dot-dash line) and 0.1×ωγWig (red long-dashed line).

scape. These include nuclear mass measurements of  Se
with precision O(100 keV), percent-level measurements
65  65
of Pβp(  Se), and studies of  Se nuclear structure as it
64
